role_summary: AFSCME (American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees) International Secretary-Treasurer; civil rights and labor organizer; co-founder of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists.
ai_reading: Lucy's top semantic neighbors are Kay Tillow (entry 74, count 116, labor-and-health-organizing voice), William Saunders (entry 130), Judge D'Army Bailey (entry 26, Memphis civil rights attorney), Phil Hutchings (entry 98, SNCC chairman 1968), and Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132, SCLC executive director). The cluster bridges labor organizing (Lucy himself, Tillow, Saunders), Memphis civil rights legal work (Bailey, who was on the bench in the city where the AFSCME-organized Memphis sanitation workers' strike of 1968 drew MLK to his death), and SNCC/SCLC leadership (Hutchings, Walker). The embedding therefore reads Lucy's testimony as the labor-civil-rights bridge his career was built on; his -0.62 Sacred Framing reading is consistent with the secular-labor-union vocabulary of his AFSCME role.
biographical_paragraph: William Lucy's oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 128 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing is pending; the strongest signal currently available for movement role is the embedding pattern visible in the AI's reading above.

sources.title: William Lucy oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
